narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong Year 1 grades placing you in the 8th decile—well above the typical student's 6th decile ranking—and solid Year 3 performance in the 6th decile, close to average. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 matches what most applicants bring, and your family falls into the middle income bracket, which is common among peers. However, your LSAT score of 30 is notably lower than the average of 37, and you've indicated plans to attend part-time, a choice only about 7% of students make. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 40% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your part-time status, which the model sees as the strongest signal of risk compared to the vast majority who go full-time. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile ranking helped pull the prediction toward success, showing the model views it as a stabilizing strength relative to others. Similarly, your excellent Year 1 decile worked in your favor, countering some concerns by highlighting early academic promise above the norm. While these top three elements—part-time plans outweighing your solid deciles—drove the decision, your overall profile shows real potential that didn't quite overcome the risks. This isn't a judgment on your abilities or background, but a data-driven call to protect the school's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, exploring full-time options if feasible, and strengthening recent grades—then reapply to show growth. Many students in similar situations succeed by addressing these key areas, and with your early strengths, you're well-positioned to improve your odds next time. Keep pushing; law school values determined applicants like you.
